record_office_terminology.land_records: County Recorder (elected official; holds deeds, mortgages, liens, plats)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk — Circuit Court (handles wills, estates, guardianships; Indiana has no separate probate court)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1900 (statewide registration of births and deaths; compliance improved ~1907)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Indiana county pages; Indiana State Library county guides; Indiana Magazine of History (IU); county histories
administrative_notes: Indiana was admitted as the 19th state on December 11, 1816. Previously Indiana Territory (1800, split from Northwest Territory) and before that part of the Northwest Territory (1787). Knox County (established 1790) is the oldest county, with Vincennes as the territorial capital. The state capital moved from Vincennes to Corydon (1813) and then to Indianapolis (1825). Indiana is a federal land state — most land was distributed through federal land offices (BLM-GLO), except for Clark's Grant (George Rogers Clark's Illinois Regiment, 150,000 acres in southeastern Indiana). Indiana had Black Codes restricting African American settlement despite being a free state under the Northwest Ordinance. The 1851 Indiana Constitution excluded African Americans from settling in the state. County Recorder handles deeds; County Clerk handles marriages, wills, and court records.

core_repositories.source_name: Indiana State Archives (Indiana Commission on Public Records)
core_repositories.description: Primary state repository. Houses state government records, county records on microfilm, vital records, military records, canal records, territorial-era records, and Native American-related records. Indiana Archives and Records Administration (IARA). Essential starting point for Indiana research.

Outstanding genealogy division with Indiana county histories, family files, cemetery records, Bible records, newspaper clippings, and the extensive Indiana Biography Index. Microfilmed county records. Manuscripts and rare books. Essential complement to the State Archives.

core_repositories.source_name: Allen County Public Library — Genealogy Center
core_repositories.repository: Allen County Public Library, Fort Wayne
core_repositories.description: Second-largest genealogy collection in the United States (after the Family History Library). Over 350,000 printed volumes, 600,000+ microforms. Extensive databases including The Periodical Source Index (PERSI). Not just Indiana — national and international collections. Essential for any genealogical research.

Major manuscript repository with family papers, business records, photographs, and Indiana-related collections. William Henry Smith Memorial Library. Indiana History Bulletin and Indiana Magazine of History. Collections on Indiana's diverse communities.

Research collections including Lilly Library (rare books and manuscripts), Indiana University Archives, and oral history collections. Significant materials on Indiana history, politics, and culture. Wylie House Museum documents early Bloomington families.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court orders), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. FamilySearch partnership with Indiana State Library and Archives provides extensive digitized county records.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, and links to digitized microfilm. Essential starting point for county-level research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Indiana newspapers including Vincennes Western Sun (1807), Indianapolis Indiana Journal, and county weeklies. Legal notices, estate settlements, land sales, and marriage announcements.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Hoosier State Chronicles — Indiana's Digital Newspaper Program
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized Indiana newspapers beyond Chronicling America. County weeklies and small-town papers that document legal notices, local news, and community events across Indiana's 92 counties. Partnership between Indiana State Library and Library of Congress.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Historical Society
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Indiana was a free state under the Northwest Ordinance (1787) but had significant African American communities. Indiana's Black Codes (1831 Act, 1851 Constitution Article XIII) restricted African American settlement and required bonds. Court records include freedom suits, Black Code enforcement, and bond registrations. Levi Coffin (Fountain City/Newport) was the 'President of the Underground Railroad.'

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: African American Community Records — Indianapolis and Gary
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Indiana Historical Society / Calumet Regional Archives, IU Northwest
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Great Migration records: African Americans moved to Indianapolis (Indiana Avenue community) and Gary (steel industry) from the South. Church records (Bethel AME, etc.), fraternal organization records, Indianapolis Recorder newspaper (1895+), and Gary American newspaper. Indiana Avenue cultural heritage records.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Indiana State Archives / County Clerks / NARA Chicago
immigration_and_naturalization.description: German immigration dominated early Indiana settlement — Dubois, Ripley, Franklin, and many other counties. Irish canal workers (Wabash and Erie Canal). Swiss colonies (Vevay, Tell City). Later Eastern European steelworkers (Gary/Lake County). Mexican immigration to Lake County (1910s+). Naturalization records at county Circuit Courts (pre-1906) and NARA Chicago (post-1906).

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA, Washington DC / Indiana State Archives
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Indiana was homeland to the Miami, Potawatomi, and Delaware (Lenape) nations. Key records: Miami removal (1846) from Wabash/Miami/Grant counties; Potawatomi Trail of Death (1838) from Marshall County to Kansas; Delaware removal from White River (1818-1821). Treaty records, annuity rolls, allotment records, and Indian agent correspondence at NARA Record Group 75. Some Miami families (Meshingomesia, Godfroy) received allotments and remained.

military_records.description: Adjutant General records, militia records, Revolutionary War/War of 1812 bounty land records, Civil War records (Indiana provided ~197,000 Union soldiers), WWI/WWII records. W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols., 1869) — published Civil War rosters. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument in Indianapolis.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers & Sailors System — Indiana
military_records.description: Database of Union soldiers from Indiana. Cross-reference with Indiana Adjutant General records and NARA pension files. Indiana raised 129 infantry regiments, 13 cavalry regiments, and 26 light artillery batteries.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Indiana
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Indiana. Most of Indiana was public domain land sold through federal land offices at Vincennes, Jeffersonville, Brookville, Terre Haute, Indianapolis, Crawfordsville, Fort Wayne, Winamac, and La Porte. Original patents record first purchaser from the government. Clark's Grant (southeastern Indiana) was a separate system for George Rogers Clark's veterans.

land_and_property.source_name: Indiana Donation Lands and Early Surveys
land_and_property.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana State Library
land_and_property.description: Early Indiana land records include donation lands (400-acre tracts for heads of families in the Vincennes and Clark areas), Congress Lands, and territorial-era surveys. Published in Gayle Thornbrough's 'The Correspondence of John Badollet and Albert Gallatin' and other Indiana Historical Bureau publications.

vital_records.source_name: Indiana State Department of Health — Vital Records
vital_records.repository: Indiana State Department of Health, Indianapolis
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1900 (statewide registration; compliance improved ~1907). For earlier events: county marriages from county formation, county death records vary, birth records from family Bibles and church records. Some cities (Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville) had earlier registration.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Indiana State Library / Indiana Historical Society / Denominational archives
church_and_cemetery.description: Methodist (dominant early denomination), Baptist, Christian Church (Disciples of Christ — strong Indiana roots), Presbyterian, Catholic, Quaker (Friends), Amish/Mennonite, and Lutheran records. Quaker records at Earlham College (Richmond). Catholic records at diocesan archives (Indianapolis, Fort Wayne-South Bend, Evansville, Lafayette, Gary). Mennonite Historical Library at Goshen College.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Indiana. Coverage varies across 92 counties. Indiana State Library holds Indiana cemetery index (card file and microfilm). Indiana Genealogical Society cemetery survey publications.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Indiana Genealogist (quarterly) and maintains online databases. County-level society network. Annual conference.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, historical sketches, and county-specific research guides for all 92 Indiana counties.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Indiana Historical Society / Polis Center, IUPUI
research_guides_and_societies.description: Authoritative reference for Indianapolis and Marion County history. Biographical entries, institutional histories, and neighborhood histories. Useful for understanding Indianapolis research context. Digital edition available through Indiana Historical Society.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Indiana has no abolished counties. However, researchers must understand the pre-statehood jurisdictional hierarchy: Knox County (1790, Northwest Territory) originally encompassed virtually all of Indiana Territory; St. Clair County (Illinois side) also had jurisdiction over some Indiana areas. Indiana Territory was created in 1800 from the Northwest Territory with Vincennes as capital. The nine original counties at statehood (1816) — Clark, Dearborn, Franklin, Gibson, Harrison, Jefferson, Knox, Perry, and Washington — plus Wayne, were parent or grandparent to all subsequent counties. Many counties were created from 'unorganized territory' labeled as 'Delaware' or 'New Purchase' following the Treaty of St. Mary's (1818) which ceded central Indiana. The 'New Purchase' counties (1821-1827) include most of central Indiana.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Indiana has no recognized burned counties. No significant courthouse fires have destroyed county records wholesale. However, some frontier-era counties (formed before 1820) may have thin early records simply because population was sparse and record-keeping infrastructure was minimal. Researchers should check Indiana State Archives and Indiana State Library for microfilmed duplicates of early county records.
